walkthrough.title: Choose Wildlife Scoring Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's get Cascadia set up. First, find the wildlife scoring cards. There are five wildlife types in the game: Bear, Elk, Salmon, Hawk, and Fox. Each type has three scoring card variants labeled A, B, and C. For your first game, we recommend using all A cards since they have the simplest scoring patterns. Pick one card per wildlife type and lay all five face up where everyone can see them.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Habitat Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now grab all eighty-five hexagonal habitat tiles. These are the chunky hex tiles showing different terrain types and wildlife icons on them. Give them a thorough shuffle face down, then form a few roughly equal face-down stacks in the center of the table. These stacks are the shared tile supply for the whole game.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Wildlife Bag
walkthrough.text: Find the cloth bag and all one hundred wooden wildlife tokens. There are twenty tokens for each of the five wildlife types: Bear, Elk, Salmon, Hawk, and Fox. Drop all one hundred tokens into the bag and give it a good shake. You will draw from this bag throughout the game to populate the shared display.

walkthrough.title: Create the Shared Display
walkthrough.text: Now let's build the central display that everyone will draft from. Flip four habitat tiles face up in a row in the middle of the table. Then reach into the bag and draw four wildlife tokens. Place one token next to each tile to form four tile-token pairs. This display is where you will shop for tiles and tokens on your turn.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Nature Token Supply
walkthrough.text: Find the pine cone shaped nature tokens. There are twenty-five in total. Set them in a small pile near the shared display where everyone can reach them. These are a special currency that let you break the tile-token pairing rules, and any unspent tokens are worth one point at the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Starter Tiles
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one of the large three-hex starter tiles. These are bigger than the regular tiles because they are actually three hexes fused together. Place your starter tile in front of you. This is the foundation of your personal landscape, and you will build outward from it by attaching tiles to its edges.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Nature Tokens
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one nature token from the supply and places it near their starter tile. This is your starting nature token. You will have chances to earn more during the game whenever an overpopulation reset is triggered in the display.

walkthrough.title: Select the First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick the youngest player to go first, or choose randomly. Play will proceed clockwise from the first player. Everyone should take one more look at the five scoring cards before you begin so you know exactly how each wildlife type scores this game. Ready? Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Cascadia is played over about twenty turns per player. On each turn, you draft a habitat tile and a wildlife token from a shared display, then add them to your personal landscape. You are building a sprawling Pacific Northwest ecosystem, trying to arrange both terrain and wildlife in the most valuable patterns. The game ends when the tile supply runs out.

walkthrough.title: Drafting a Tile-Token Pair
walkthrough.text: On your turn, look at the four tile-token pairs in the shared display. Pick one pair and take both the habitat tile and the wildlife token paired with it. This is free and costs nothing. The trick is that you often want the tile from one pair but the token from another.

walkthrough.title: Placing Your Habitat Tile
walkthrough.text: Take the habitat tile you just drafted and add it to your landscape. The only rule is that the new tile must share at least one edge with a tile already in your landscape. You do not need to match terrain types on adjacent edges. However, connecting tiles of the same terrain type creates corridors that score bonus points at the end of the game, so matching edges is very rewarding.

walkthrough.title: Placing Your Wildlife Token
walkthrough.text: Now place the wildlife token you drafted. Look at the wildlife icons printed on each tile in your landscape. You can place the token on any tile that shows that wildlife type's icon and does not already have a token on it. Each tile can hold at most one wildlife token. If you cannot or do not want to place the token, return it to the bag instead.

walkthrough.title: Refilling the Display
walkthrough.text: After you have placed your tile and token, refill the shared display back to four pairs. Flip a new habitat tile face up from the supply stacks and draw a new wildlife token from the bag. Pair them together and add them to the display. Now the next player takes their turn.

walkthrough.title: Using Nature Tokens
walkthrough.text: Here is where nature tokens become powerful. Instead of taking a matched tile-token pair, you can spend one nature token to draft any tile and any token from the display independently. This breaks the pairing, letting you grab the exact combination you need. Unspent nature tokens are worth one victory point each at the end of the game, so only spend them when the value gained clearly exceeds one point.

walkthrough.title: Overpopulation Reset
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the display gets flooded with the same wildlife type. If at any point three or more wildlife tokens in the display are the same type, an overpopulation reset is triggered. Return all four wildlife tokens to the bag, then draw four fresh tokens to repopulate the display. Every player receives one nature token from the supply as a bonus. This can happen during the refill step or at any point the display is checked.

walkthrough.title: Habitat Tile Details
walkthrough.text: Each habitat tile is a hexagon showing one or two terrain types. There are five terrain types in the game: Mountains, Forests, Prairies, Wetlands, and Rivers. The tile also shows one to three wildlife icons indicating which animals can be placed on it. Tiles with only one wildlife icon are called keystone tiles. They are restrictive but helpful for guaranteeing placement of a specific animal type.

walkthrough.title: Wildlife Scoring Cards
walkthrough.text: Each wildlife type scores according to its scoring card. Bears typically score for groups of specific sizes. Elk typically score for straight lines of adjacent tokens. Salmon typically score for continuous runs that do not loop back on themselves. Hawks typically score for being isolated, meaning no other hawk is adjacent. Foxes typically score based on the variety of other wildlife types adjacent to each fox. Always check your specific cards since the A, B, and C variants change these patterns.

walkthrough.title: Habitat Corridor Bonuses
walkthrough.text: At the end of the game, you score bonus points for terrain corridors. A corridor is a group of connected tiles sharing the same terrain type. For each of the five terrain types, the player with the single largest contiguous group scores bonus points. These are majority bonuses: if two players tie for the largest group of a terrain type, both receive the full bonus. There are five terrain types, so there are five separate majority comparisons.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends when there are not enough habitat tiles left to refill the display. Each player finishes their final turn, then you tally scores. Add up points from all five wildlife scoring cards, then add habitat corridor bonuses for each terrain type where you have the largest group, then add one point for each unspent nature token. The player with the most total points wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most remaining nature tokens wins.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: ~20 turns per player, draft tile + token each turn
Build a personal landscape of terrain and wildlife
Game ends when tile supply runs out

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Pacific Northwest
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Cascadia. In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the opening turns with three example players. You will see how drafting, tile placement, and wildlife placement work in practice, and you will watch one player make common beginner mistakes so you can learn from them. Follow along even if you have more or fewer players at your table.

walkthrough.title: Read the Scoring Cards First
walkthrough.text: Before anyone drafts, every player should study the five wildlife scoring cards on the table. Understanding how each animal scores is the single most important thing in Cascadia. Take a minute right now to read each card carefully. Note which animals need groups, which need lines, and which need isolation or variety.

walkthrough.title: Player One Drafts Wisely
walkthrough.text: Player One looks at the four tile-token pairs in the display. They notice a Mountain tile paired with a Hawk token. Their landscape already has a Mountain hex on the starter tile, and the Hawk scoring card rewards isolated hawks. They take this pair, connect the Mountain tile to their existing Mountain hex to start building a corridor, and place the Hawk on a tile with plenty of empty space around it. Good start.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was Smart
walkthrough.text: Player One accomplished two goals with one draft. They extended a Mountain corridor for the habitat bonus, and they placed a Hawk in an isolated spot to match its scoring pattern. Every turn in Cascadia should serve at least one scoring goal, and ideally two. Always think about both the tile and the token before you choose a pair.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Drafts a Token They Need
walkthrough.text: Player Two sees a Bear token paired with a Prairie tile. They really want that Bear because their scoring card rewards pairs of Bears, and they already have one Bear placed. The Prairie tile is not ideal for their terrain plans, but the Bear is worth more than a slightly better tile. They take the pair, place the Prairie tile on the edge of their landscape, and put the Bear adjacent to their first one to form a scoring pair.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Makes a Mistake: Random Placement
walkthrough.text: Player Three grabs a Forest tile paired with an Elk token. They place the Forest tile next to a Wetland with no matching terrain, then drop the Elk token on the first available hex without thinking. The Elk lands in a corner surrounded by other wildlife types, with no room to extend a line. But the Elk scoring card rewards straight lines of adjacent Elk tokens. That Elk is now stranded and will score nothing.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Place Wildlife with a Plan
walkthrough.text: Every wildlife token you place should serve the scoring card's pattern. Before placing an Elk, ask yourself where a straight line can grow. Before placing a Salmon, trace a potential run. Before placing a Hawk, make sure no other Hawk is adjacent. Dropping tokens randomly wastes your biggest source of points. Always look at the scoring card, then place deliberately.

walkthrough.title: Player One Extends the Corridor
walkthrough.text: On their second turn, Player One takes another tile with Mountain terrain and connects it to their growing Mountain corridor. That is three Mountain hexes in a row now. They are investing early in the habitat majority bonus. The wildlife token this turn is a Fox, which they place next to two different wildlife types to start building variety for the Fox scoring card.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Spends a Nature Token
walkthrough.text: Player Two sees a Salmon token they desperately need, but it is paired with a tile that does not help them at all. Meanwhile, a different tile in the display would extend their River corridor beautifully. Player Two spends their nature token to take the River tile and the Salmon token independently. This costs them one nature token, but the scoring value they gain from both the corridor and the Salmon run far exceeds the one point they gave up.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Makes a Mistake: Spending Nature Tokens Frivolously
walkthrough.text: Player Three also decides to spend a nature token, but for a much weaker reason. They spend it to swap a Hawk token for a Bear token even though they have no Bears placed yet and no plan for Bear scoring. They just thought Bears looked nicer. That nature token was worth one guaranteed point at end of game, and the swap gained them almost nothing.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Spend Nature Tokens Only When the Payoff Is Clear
walkthrough.text: Nature tokens are worth one point if you never spend them. Only use one when the independent draft will clearly gain you more than one point. Good reasons include completing a wildlife scoring pattern or connecting a terrain corridor that wins you the majority bonus. Bad reasons include vague preferences or swapping for a token you have no plan to score.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Makes a Mistake: Ignoring Habitat Corridors
walkthrough.text: Three turns in, Player Three has five terrain types scattered across their landscape with no two matching hexes touching each other. They have been picking tiles based only on the wildlife token, completely ignoring terrain connections. Meanwhile, Player One has a cluster of four Mountains and Player Two has a string of three Rivers. Player Three will score zero habitat bonuses at this rate.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Terrain Corridors Are Not Optional
walkthrough.text: Habitat corridor bonuses can easily add ten to fifteen points to your final score. When you draft a tile, look at both the wildlife token and the terrain types. Try to connect tiles of the same terrain whenever possible. You do not need to win every terrain majority, but winning two or three of the five can be the difference between winning and losing.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Makes a Mistake: Tunnel Vision on Bears
walkthrough.text: After a few more turns, Player Three has placed four Bears in a cluster and is thrilled about the Bear scoring card. But they have completely neglected the other four wildlife types. They have zero Salmon, one random Hawk next to another Hawk, and no Foxes at all. The other players are scoring across all five wildlife types while Player Three is earning big Bear points but leaving twenty or more points from other animals on the table.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Score Across All Five Wildlife Types
walkthrough.text: It is tempting to go all in on one animal, but Cascadia rewards breadth. Every wildlife type has scoring potential, and ignoring even one type means leaving easy points behind. You do not need to maximize every animal, but placing a few tokens of each type in their correct pattern is much better than perfecting one type while ignoring the rest.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Recovery
walkthrough.text: Player Three changes course. On their next few turns, they start placing Elk tokens in a line, put a Hawk in an isolated spot, and begin a Salmon run. They also start connecting Forest tiles to build a terrain corridor. It is not too late to recover. Their Bear cluster is still strong, and now they are picking up points from other sources too.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on completing the practice tutorial. You have seen smart drafting, deliberate wildlife placement, terrain corridor building, and nature token management. You have also watched Player Three learn from four common mistakes: random wildlife placement, frivolous nature token spending, ignoring habitat corridors, and tunnel vision on one animal. You are ready to play a full game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Explore
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to wildlife scoring patterns, terrain types, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for scoring and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, placing wildlife tokens randomly instead of following the scoring card patterns. Second, ignoring habitat corridor bonuses and scattering terrain types everywhere. Third, spending nature tokens on vague preferences instead of saving them or using them for clear multi-point gains. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Study the Scoring Cards Before Drafting
walkthrough.text: The most important thing you can do before your first turn is to memorize how each wildlife type scores this game. If Bears score for pairs, you need to plan placement in twos. If Elk score for lines, you need to leave room for a straight path. If Hawks score for isolation, you need empty buffer zones. Knowing the scoring patterns drives every single decision you make, from which pair you draft to where you place the tile and token.

walkthrough.title: Balance Tile Value and Token Value
walkthrough.text: Every draft is a two-part decision. Sometimes the tile is the star because it extends a crucial terrain corridor. Sometimes the token is the star because it completes a scoring pattern. The best drafts serve both goals at once. When you are scanning the four pairs in the display, ask yourself two questions: which tile helps my terrain the most, and which token helps my wildlife patterns the most? If the answer is the same pair, take it immediately. If not, weigh which scoring opportunity is worth more points.

walkthrough.title: Invest in Terrain Corridors Early
walkthrough.text: Habitat corridor bonuses reward the player with the largest connected group of each terrain type. These bonuses can easily add ten to fifteen points to your total. The key is to start building corridors in the first few turns, not as an afterthought near the end. Pick two or three terrain types to focus on and try to connect tiles of those types whenever you draft. You do not need to win all five terrain majorities, but winning two or three is a major scoring advantage.

walkthrough.title: Spread Your Wildlife Across All Five Types
walkthrough.text: It is tempting to go deep on one or two wildlife types, but Cascadia rewards breadth. Each scoring card offers points, and ignoring even one type means leaving easy points on the table. Aim to place at least a few tokens of every wildlife type in positions that match their scoring pattern. You do not need to maximize every type, but a few well-placed tokens of each type will outscore a strategy that perfects one type and ignores the rest.

walkthrough.title: Nature Token Strategy
walkthrough.text: Nature tokens are worth one point each if you never spend them, but they can be worth much more when used at the right moment. The best time to spend a nature token is when the independent draft will gain you three or more points, such as completing a Salmon run, finishing a Bear pair, or grabbing the exact tile that wins a terrain majority. If the gain is marginal, save the token. Over the course of a game, two or three unspent tokens add up to meaningful points.

walkthrough.title: Watch What Other Players Are Building
walkthrough.text: Cascadia is primarily a personal puzzle, but you should keep an eye on your opponents. If another player is building a massive Forest corridor, you might not want to compete for Forest majority. Instead, invest in a terrain type they are ignoring. Similarly, if someone is scooping up all the Bear tokens, shift your focus to other wildlife types. You do not need to hate-draft often, but awareness of what others are collecting helps you make better decisions about where to invest your efforts.

walkthrough.title: Plan Tile Placement for Future Flexibility
walkthrough.text: When placing a habitat tile, think about what future tiles could connect to it. Avoid painting yourself into a corner by leaving only one open edge on your landscape. Try to keep multiple open edges available, especially edges that continue your terrain corridors. A landscape that grows in a compact shape with many connection points gives you more options than a long skinny chain that limits where new tiles can go.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't place wildlife randomly — follow the scoring card patterns
Don't ignore habitat corridors — they're worth 10-15 points
Don't waste nature tokens on vague preferences — save or spend big

Scoring knowledge drives every drafting and placement decision

Corridor bonuses can add 10-15 points to final score

Breadth > depth: well-placed tokens across 5 types beats perfecting 1
Ignoring any wildlife type leaves easy points on the table

summary.title: Nature Token Strategy
bullets: Unspent tokens = 1 VP each (guaranteed floor)
Spend only when gain clearly exceeds 1 VP (aim for 3+ points)
Good uses: completing scoring patterns, winning terrain majorities

summary.title: Watch What Other Players Are Building

If someone is hoarding a wildlife type → shift to others
Awareness helps you avoid unwinnable competitions

summary.title: Plan Tile Placement for Future Flexibility
bullets: Keep multiple open edges on your landscape
Compact shapes with many connection points > long skinny chains
Leave room for future corridor extensions

walkthrough.title: Terrain Majority Control
walkthrough.text: Habitat corridor bonuses are one of the biggest swing factors in Cascadia. An advanced player does not just grow corridors randomly. They track the largest group each opponent has per terrain type and invest only where they can win or tie. Winning three terrain majorities can net you twelve or more bonus points while denying those same points to opponents. If you see an opponent building a huge River corridor, consider whether matching it or abandoning River for a less-contested terrain type is more efficient.

walkthrough.title: Keystone Tile Drafting
walkthrough.text: Keystone tiles show only one wildlife icon, which makes them restrictive but strategically powerful. When you place a token on a keystone tile, you know exactly which animal will go there, eliminating guesswork. Draft keystone tiles early for wildlife types that need precise placement, like Salmon runs or Elk lines. Keystone tiles also tend to appear less frequently, so grabbing them when they show up in the display keeps your opponents from using them.

walkthrough.title: Salmon Run Optimization
walkthrough.text: Salmon scoring typically rewards long continuous runs of adjacent Salmon tokens that do not loop. The key to maximizing Salmon is to plan the run's path before you start placing tokens. Leave a clear channel of tiles with Salmon icons extending in one direction. Avoid placing Salmon tokens that create branches or loops since these can invalidate the run or cut it short. A single run of five or six Salmon can be one of the highest-scoring achievements in the game.

walkthrough.title: Hawk Spacing Mastery
walkthrough.text: Hawk scoring typically rewards hawks that are not adjacent to any other hawk. This sounds simple but becomes tricky as your landscape fills up. The advanced move is to plan Hawk positions two or three tiles ahead. Place Hawks on tiles that are separated by at least one hex in every direction. Use the buffer hexes between Hawks for other wildlife types. A common mistake is placing a Hawk and then accidentally placing another Hawk adjacent to it later because you forgot where the first one was.

walkthrough.title: Fox Adjacency Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Fox scoring typically rewards having many different wildlife types adjacent to each Fox. This means Foxes want to sit in the middle of your landscape surrounded by Bears, Elk, Salmon, and Hawks. The advanced technique is to create central hub positions specifically for Foxes. Place other wildlife types around the perimeter first, leaving a central tile open for a Fox that can see three or four different animal types. A Fox surrounded by four unique wildlife types can score as many points as an entire Elk line.

walkthrough.title: Display Manipulation and Denial
walkthrough.text: In a multiplayer game, the shared display creates indirect interaction. Advanced players track what opponents need and sometimes draft a pair specifically to deny it. If an opponent needs one more Elk to complete a high-scoring line, taking that Elk token even if it is less useful to you can be a net positive move. This is especially valuable in two-player games where denial is more impactful. In three or four player games, focus more on your own scoring since denial is diluted.

walkthrough.title: Nature Token Counting
walkthrough.text: Advanced players count nature tokens carefully. Each unspent token is one point, which seems small but adds up. In a close game, the player with three unspent nature tokens versus zero has a three-point edge. Only spend a nature token when the independent draft will net you at least three more points than the best paired draft available. Keep mental track of how many tokens each player has, since the overpopulation reset grants tokens to everyone equally.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Card Variant Mastery
walkthrough.text: The A, B, and C scoring card variants dramatically change how each wildlife type scores. Advanced players study all three variants for each type and adjust their strategy based on the specific combination in play. For example, the B or C Bear card might reward groups of three instead of pairs, completely changing how you cluster Bears. Before the game starts, read each of the five selected cards carefully and mentally map out how your landscape should look to maximize all five simultaneously. The best players have a rough scoring blueprint in mind by turn three.

summary.title: Terrain Majority Control
bullets: Track opponents' largest terrain groups per type
Invest only where you can win or tie the majority
Winning 3 majorities → 12+ bonus VP
Abandon contested terrains for uncontested ones

summary.title: Keystone Tile Drafting
bullets: Single-icon tiles guarantee specific wildlife placement
Draft early for types needing precise placement (Salmon, Elk)
Denies opponents access to restricted-placement tiles

summary.title: Salmon Run Optimization
bullets: Plan the run's path before placing tokens
Leave a clear channel of Salmon-icon tiles in one direction
Avoid branches or loops that invalidate the run
5-6 Salmon run = one of the highest-scoring achievements

summary.title: Hawk Spacing Mastery
bullets: Plan Hawk positions 2-3 tiles ahead
Separate Hawks by at least 1 hex in every direction
Use buffer hexes for other wildlife types
Track placed Hawks to avoid accidental adjacency

summary.title: Fox Adjacency Manipulation
bullets: Create central hub positions for Foxes
Surround Fox tiles with 3-4 unique wildlife types
Place perimeter wildlife first, save central spot for Fox
1 well-placed Fox can rival an entire Elk line in points

summary.title: Display Manipulation and Denial
bullets: Track what opponents need from the display
Deny critical tokens (e.g., Elk to complete a line)
More impactful in 2-player; less so in 3-4 player
Prioritize own scoring over denial in most cases

summary.title: Nature Token Counting
bullets: Each unspent token = 1 VP; 3 tokens = 3-point edge
Only spend when independent draft nets 3+ points over best pair
Track opponents' token counts for end-game projections
Overpopulation resets grant tokens to all players equally

summary.title: Scoring Card Variant Mastery
bullets: A/B/C variants drastically change wildlife scoring patterns
Study all 5 selected cards before turn 1
Mentally map ideal landscape by turn 3
Adjust Bear clusters, Elk lines, etc. to the specific variant in play

entries.definition: A hexagonal tile showing one or two terrain types and one to three wildlife icons. Players draft and place these tiles to build their personal landscape. Eighty-five tiles in the base game.
A wooden token representing one of the five wildlife types. Drawn from the cloth bag and placed on habitat tiles that show the matching wildlife icon. One hundred tokens total, twenty per type.

A pine cone token that can be spent to draft any tile and any token independently, breaking the normal pairing rule. Each unspent nature token is worth one victory point at game end. Players start with one each.
The central area showing four habitat tiles each paired with one wildlife token. Players draft one pair per turn. The display is refilled after each draft.
A larger tile consisting of three connected hexes given to each player at the start. It forms the foundation of your landscape and includes various terrain types and wildlife icons.
A card that defines how one wildlife type scores. There are three variants per type (A, B, C) with increasing complexity. One card per wildlife type is selected each game.
One of five wildlife types. Typically scores for groups of specific sizes, such as pairs. Bear tokens are placed on tiles showing the bear icon.
One of five wildlife types. Typically scores for straight lines of adjacent elk tokens. Longer lines score exponentially more points.
One of five wildlife types. Typically scores for continuous runs of adjacent salmon tokens that do not loop back on themselves. Longer non-branching runs score more.
One of five wildlife types. Typically scores for isolation, meaning each hawk earns points only if no other hawk is adjacent to it.
One of five wildlife types. Typically scores based on the variety of unique wildlife types adjacent to each fox token. More variety means more points per fox.
One of five terrain types shown on habitat tiles. Forms corridors when adjacent mountain hexes are connected. Largest mountain group scores a habitat bonus.
One of five terrain types shown on habitat tiles. Connected forest hexes form corridors eligible for the habitat majority bonus.
One of five terrain types shown on habitat tiles. Connected prairie hexes form corridors eligible for the habitat majority bonus.
One of five terrain types shown on habitat tiles. Connected wetland hexes form corridors eligible for the habitat majority bonus.
One of five terrain types shown on habitat tiles. Connected river hexes form corridors eligible for the habitat majority bonus.

A contiguous group of connected hexes sharing the same terrain type. At game end, the player with the largest corridor per terrain type scores bonus victory points. Ties award the full bonus to all tied players.

Bonus points awarded for having the largest contiguous group of each terrain type. There are five separate comparisons, one per terrain type. Typical bonus values range from one to three points per terrain type depending on player count.

The specific arrangement of wildlife tokens that scores points according to the active scoring card. Patterns include groups, lines, runs, isolation, and adjacency variety depending on the wildlife type and card variant.

Triggered when three or more wildlife tokens of the same type appear in the shared display. All four tokens are returned to the bag and four new tokens are drawn. Every player receives one nature token as a bonus.

A habitat tile showing only one wildlife icon. More restrictive for token placement but guarantees that a specific wildlife type can be placed there. Strategically useful for types needing precise positioning.

When a player spends a nature token to take any habitat tile and any wildlife token from the display independently, ignoring the normal pairing. Allows maximum drafting flexibility at the cost of one victory point.

Each wildlife type has three scoring card versions: A (simplest), B (moderate), and C (advanced with higher scoring ceilings). Players can use all of one letter or mix letters across different wildlife types for variety.

Hexagonal tiles have six edges. A new tile must share at least one edge with an existing tile in your landscape. Terrain types on adjacent edges do not need to match, but matching terrain forms corridors.
